rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE THE MATILDA CYTRON TRUST U/D/T DATED MARCH 20, 1978, as amended
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion For Judgement On The Pleadings (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 4] The Petition alleges Respondent breached her duty of trust by: failing to follow the terms of the Trust, as amended in 1998 (Pet. at par. 49); using all Trust income for the benefit of her mother, and to the detriment of the other beneficiaries, beginning in 2010 (Pet. at par. 36, 50); that she failed to notify the beneficiaries when she began serving as the trustee in 2010 (Pet. at par. 51); failed to account to the beneficiaries at any time (Pet. at par. 53); engaged in self-dealing when she sold the Trust real property in April 2022 and received a commission on the sale (Pet. at par. 33, 54); failed to provide a Notice of Proposed Action regarding paying herself a commission on the sale (Pet.at par. 54); used over $80,000 dollars of Trust funds for personal expenses between 2016 and 2023 (Pet. at par. 55); and failed to distribute non-Trust funds to the beneficiaries' personal shares, instead comingling the shares with Trust funds (Pet. at par. 34, 51, 56). The Motion asserts the Petition's allegations and the judicially noticed court records show Petitioner knew or should have discovered the subject of her Third Cause of Action for breach of trust by 1998 when the Trust was amended, again in 2007 when Petitioner's mother, as a beneficiary of the Trust, died and her estate was administered and distributed, and finally "at all times between 2007 and 2022." (Motion at 3:8-14.) The Motion relies heavily on the contents of the judicially noticed documents, particularly Consent of Beneficiaries and its reference to the Petition for Appointment of Successor Trustee; Ascertaining Beneficiaries. "Judicial notice" is the court's recognition of the existence of a matter of law or fact, without the necessity of formal proof of the existence of a matter of law or fact. (See Evid. C. section 450 et seq.) Judicial notice is limited to the relevant facts properly subject to notice given the materials presented and does not extend automatically to the truth of assertions in those materials. (See Richtek USA, Inc. v. UPI Semiconductor Corp. (2015) 242 Cal.App.4th 651, 659-660.) "A court may take judicial notice of the existence of each document in a court file, but can only take judicial notice of the truth of facts asserted in documents such as orders, findings of fact and conclusions of law, and judgments. [citation.] [ ] A mistaken notion exists that taking judicial notice of court records means noticing [ ] the existence of facts asserted in [all the documents in] a court file[.] [A] court cannot take judicial notice of hearsay allegations as being true, just because they are part of a court record or file[.]" (Garcia v. Sterling (1985) 176 Cal. App. 3d 17, 21-22, quoting Day v. Sharp (1975) 50 Cal.App.3d 904, 914, emphasis in original.) It is improper to rely on judicially noticed documents to prove disputed facts because judicial notice, by definition, applies solely to undisputed facts. (See Cal. Evid. Code sec. 452, 459; Barri v. Workers' Comp. Appeals Bd. (2018) 28 Cal. App. 5th 428, 437.) Of the four judicially noticed court records, there is only one order: Order for Appointment of Successor Trustee and Determining Beneficiaries to Whom Property Shall Pass Upon Final Termination of the Trust, filed December 3, 1998. Neither the Motion, the face of the Petition, nor the judicially noticed documents demonstrate how the 1998 Order's contents show Petitioner knew or should have discovered the factual basis for her breach of duty claims against Respondent in 1998, when the alleged breaches began at the earliest in 2010 when Respondent began acting as trustee in her mother's stead.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE THE MATILDA CYTRON TRUST U/D/T DATED MARCH 20, 1978, as amended
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion For Judgement On The Pleadings
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 4] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Ross C. Moody. Before the Court is Respondent Sally Gati's Motion for Judgment on the Pleadings. The court GRANTS movant's requests for judicial notice of four documents, facially court records of the Superior Court of California, Los Angeles County related to The Matilda Cytron Trust dated March 20, 1978, under Evidence Code sections 452(d) and 453. Respondent Sally Gati's Motion for Judgment on the Pleadings is DENIED, for the following reasons. A motion for judgment on the pleadings performs the same function as a general demurrer, and hence attacks only defects disclosed on the face of the pleadings or by matters that can be judicially noticed. (See, Code of Civil Procedure section 438(d); Spencer v. City of Palos Verdes Estates (2023) 88 Cal. App. 5th 849, 861-862.) As on a general demurrer, the court accepts the complaint's properly pleaded factual allegations as true and gives them a liberal construction. (Spencer v. City of Palos Verdes Estates (2023) 88 Cal. App. 5th 849.) Respondent moves for judgment on the pleadings as to the Third Cause of Action in Petition 1) For Order Determining Questions of Interpretation of Trust [Probate Code sec. 17200(b)(1)] and Instructing Trustee to Follow Terms of Trust; 2) For Order Compelling Trustee To Account In Accordance With Probate Code Sections 1060-1064; 3) For Redress of Breach of Trust 4) For Order Removing Trustee; 5) Order Allowing Attorney Fees and Costs ("Petition"), on grounds that the breach of trust claims are barred by the three year statute of limitations under Probate Code section 16460. Probate Code section 16460 provides a three-year limitation on beneficiaries asserting a claim against the trustee for breach of trust, after adequate disclosure. Here, the Petition alleges that Petitioner has never received a trust accounting or trustee activity report, and beneficiaries have never received an accounting, "even after written demand by Petitioner." (Petition at par. 44, 53.) Thus, the statutory limitation on Petition's breach of trust claim, for the purposes of this Motion, falls under Section 16460(a)(2): "[T]he claim is barred as to that beneficiary unless a proceeding to assert the claim is commenced within three years after the beneficiary discovered, or reasonably should have discovered, the subject of the claim." The Petition was filed August 28, 2023. "When liberally construing a pleading [for the purpose of a motion for judgment on the pleadings], courts give [the challenged pleading's] allegations an objectively reasonable interpretation, reading it as a whole and its parts in their context." (Davis v. Fresno Unified School District (2020) 57 Cal. App. 5th 911, 926.) To that end, the court notes the following: On the face of the 1998 Order submitted for judicial notice, Respondent's mother, Shirley Cytron, was co-trustee and Leonard Horowitz was appointed successor co-trustee of the Trust as of December 3, 1998. The Petition facially alleges Petitioner's mother, a Trust beneficiary, died in 2007 (Pet. at par. 23, 32, 34, 43). The Petition alleges Respondent Gati began acting as co-trustee in 2010, when she began managing and controlling the trust on behalf of her mother (Pet. par. 4, 10, 43, 50, 52), and she performs the Trust's day-to-day administration (Pet. at par. 5). The Petition alleges the surviving beneficiary, Respondent's mother, died December 3, 2019 (Pet. at par. 11). The Petition alleges Respondent, acting as trustee, sold the rental-income generating Trust real property on April 6, 2022 (Pet. par 33, 54). The Petition alleges Petitioner, through counsel, requested Respondent provide a trust accounting in compliance with Probate Code sections 16063, 1060-1064, on March 16, 2023. Petitioner received a response from Respondent, through counsel, on May 23, 2023.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE THE MATILDA CYTRON TRUST U/D/T DATED MARCH 20, 1978, as amended
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion For Judgement On The Pleadings (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 4 of 4] On the face of the Petition as a whole and the judicially noticed documents, Petitioner knew Respondent was acting as trustee, and that she had not received a formal accounting, at least as of March 2023. Under an objective reading of the Petition's allegations, the basis for the other breach of trust claims were discovered in the May 23, 2023 document production. The Petition was filed August 28, 2023. On the record before the court, under the analysis on a motion for judgment on the pleadings, the Third Cause of Action's breach of trust claims cannot be declared time barred. Whether Petitioner will be able to support the Petition's claims with sufficient evidence, and whether Respondent will be able to rebut or defend against these claims with a countervailing showing of sufficient evidence, is not before the court on this Motion. The moving party shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling as published above. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765# and passcode 514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE THE MATILDA CYTRON TRUST U/D/T DATED MARCH 20, 1978, as amended
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion For Judgement On The Pleadings (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 3 of 4] Respondent's argument in the Reply, that the court may take judicial notice of the "facts that clearly derive from legal effect" of the 1998 Order, the Consent of Beneficiaries document, and the Petition for Appointment of Successor Trustee; Ascertaining Beneficiaries, is unavailing. As stated in the case Movant cites, "[T]he fact may be judicially noticed where, as here, the fact is not reasonably subject to dispute." (Scott v. JPMorgan Chase Bank, N.A. (2013) 214 Cal.App.4th 743, 754, emphasis added.) Per the Opposition to this Motion, the fact of when Petitioner knew or should have discovered the alleged breaches of trust is very much in dispute. Indeed, the legal meaning and effect of the Trust amendment ordered in 1998 is overtly in dispute as the subject of the Petition's First Cause of Action for court interpretation of the distributive provisions of the Trust as amended and instructions to the Trustee thereon (see, Petition at par.14-18, 22). Judgment on the pleadings must be denied where there are material factual issues that require evidentiary resolution. (Schabarum v. California Legislature (1998) 60 Cal. App. 4th 1205, 1216, citing Bach v. McNelis (1998) 207 Cal. App. 3d 852, 864-66.) Contrary to the Motion's additional argument that Petitioner should have been aware of the basis for the breach of trust claims when Petitioner's mother died in 2007, there is no showing on the face of the Petition or the judicially noticed documents that the beneficiary's estate was probated or distributed, or that the event of her death in 2007 should have alerted Petitioner to the breaches that allegedly began in 2010. The Motion's final argument is that Petitioner was on inquiry notice between 2007 and 2022, when her mother died and Petitioner should have received Trust income. This argument ignores the issue embodied in the Petition's First Cause of Action: administration of the Trust as amended is disputed, and with it when, how much, and to whom distributions of income generated from the Trust real property should have been made or should be made now. There is no showing on the face of the pleading or in the judicially noticed documents that Petitioner expected to begin receiving Trust income when Respondent's mother became the surviving beneficiary under Paragraph 5 of the amended Trust in 2007. The Petition states at paragraph 23, "since Lenora passed away before Shirley, Shirley became entitled to 100% of the income of the trust estate, as per Paragraph 5." The Petition alleges on its face Respondent began acting as trustee in 2010 and began breaching the duty of trust at that time. There is no objectively reasonable correlation to be drawn from the face of the Petition or the judicially noticed documents, connecting what Movant argues is the "legal effect" of the 1998 documents and the factual issue of when Petitioner knew or should have discovered the basis of the Third Cause of Action's breach of trust claims against Respondent. What Petitioner knew or should have discovered in 1998 about the Trust as amended, or what Petitioner knew or should have discovered about how the Trust as amended was being administered in 2007, is irrelevant to the timeliness of Petitioner's claim that Respondent began acting as trustee in 2010 and has been breaching various duties of trust ever since.
